analysis: Derived Domain-Specific KGs vs Parent SexDiffKG v4
author: J.Shaik

insights.finding: Domain-specific sub-KGs consistently outperform the parent general KG (DistMult)
insights.detail: All 3 derived KGs achieve higher MRR than the parent DistMult (range 0.1277-0.1629 vs 0.1013), despite using identical architecture (DistMult 200d, 100 epochs).
MRR 0.1629 (+60.8% vs parent DistMult), despite being the smallest sub-KG (13,208 entities, 384,985 triples). Smallest graph = highest signal-to-noise ratio.

insights.finding: Signal-to-noise ratio hypothesis supported by entity-triple ratios
insights.detail: Derived KGs have entity-per-triple ratios of 0.0343, 0.0249, 0.0254 vs parent's 0.0603. The lower ratio (fewer entities relative to triples) in the parent means more noise for the embedding model to learn through.

insights.finding: ComplEx on the parent KG still dominates overall
insights.detail: SexDiffKG v4 ComplEx (MRR 0.2484) outperforms all derived DistMult models, showing that model architecture matters more than domain filtering for absolute performance. The ideal strategy would be ComplEx on domain-specific sub-KGs.

insights.finding: MRR improvement inversely correlates with sub-KG size
insights.detail: REPRODUCT-KG (smallest, 385K triples): +60.8% MRR. MENTALHEALTH-KG (mid, 706K triples): +26.1% MRR. GERIPHARM-KG (largest, 739K triples): +42.0% MRR. Smaller, more focused graphs yield proportionally larger gains.

insights.finding: AMRI remains high across all models (>0.95)
insights.detail: Adjusted Mean Rank Index exceeds 0.95 for all models, confirming that predictions are far better than random for every configuration. The parent KG has slightly higher AMRI (0.9909) likely because its larger entity space makes random baseline worse.

methodology_note: All derived KGs were extracted as domain-specific subsets of SexDiffKG v4 (109,867 entities, 1,822,851 triples) and trained with identical DistMult hyperparameters (200d embeddings, 100 epochs) to enable fair same-architecture comparison. ComplEx results on the parent KG are included as an upper-bound reference.
